Chapter 3 Bayesian Modeling in R and Stan: Categorical Predictors and Interactions 3.1 Simulating the Data 3.2 Stan Model File 3.3 Fitting the Model 3.4 MCMC Diagnostics 3.5 Interpreting Interaction Effects 3.6 Comparing with rstanarm 3.7 Conclusions 3.8 References

Chapter 3 Bayesian Modeling in R and Stan: Categorical Predictors and Interactions In the previous chapter, we introduced Bayesian linear regression using Stan and rstan with continuous predictors. Here, we extend that framework to include a categorical predictor with three levels and its interactions with continuous predictors, so that each continuous slope varies by group. 3.1 Simulating the Data We simulate a dataset where a categorical variable g (with three levels: 1, 2, 3) moderates the effect of two continuous predictors x1 and x2. This means each group gets its own intercept and its own slopes for x1 and x2. set.seed(42) n <- 900 # 300 per group # Categorical variable (3 levels, dummy-coded below) g <- rep(1:3, each = 300) # Continuous predictors x1 <- rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 1) x2 <- rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 1) # True parameters alpha <- c(10, 20, 30) # group-specific intercepts beta1 <- c(2.0, -1.5, 0.5) # group-specific slopes for x1 beta2 <- c(1.0, 3.0, -2.0) # group-specific slopes for x2 sigma_true <- 3.0 # Generate outcome y <- alpha[g] + beta1[g] * x1 + beta2[g] * x2 + rnorm(n, 0, sigma_true) data <- data.frame(y, x1, x2, g = factor(g)) head(data) %>% kableExtra::kable() This design ensures we have group-specific intercepts and slopes — equivalent to a full interaction between g and each continuous predictor. The true parameters are clearly defined so we can later verify that Stan recovers them accurately. 3.2 Stan Model File The key difference from the previous post is that instead of a single alpha and beta vector, we now define arrays indexed by group (alpha[K], beta1[K], beta2[K]). The model block uses the integer group index g[i] to select the appropriate parameters for each observation. stan_mod_cat = " data { int<lower=0> N; // number of observations int<lower=1> K; // number of groups (= 3) vector[N] y; // outcome vector[N] x1; // continuous predictor 1 vector[N] x2; // continuous predictor 2 array[N] int<lower=1, upper=K> g; // group indicator (1, 2, or 3) } parameters { vector[K] alpha; // group-specific intercepts vector[K] beta1; // group-specific slopes for x1 vector[K] beta2; // group-specific slopes for x2 real<lower=0> sigma; // shared residual SD } model { // Weakly informative priors alpha ~ normal(0, 50); beta1 ~ normal(0, 10); beta2 ~ normal(0, 10); sigma ~ exponential(1); // Likelihood with group-specific intercepts and slopes for (i in 1:N) { y[i] ~ normal(alpha[g[i]] + beta1[g[i]] * x1[i] + beta2[g[i]] * x2[i], sigma); } } generated quantities { vector[N] y_rep; // posterior predictive samples for (i in 1:N) y_rep[i] = normal_rng(alpha[g[i]] + beta1[g[i]] * x1[i] + beta2[g[i]] * x2[i], sigma); } " writeLines(stan_mod_cat, con = "stan_mod_cat.stan") cat(stan_mod_cat) The generated quantities block is new here — it produces posterior predictive samples (y_rep), which are useful for posterior predictive checks to assess model fit. 3.3 Fitting the Model We build the Stan data list, making sure to pass g as an integer vector (not a factor), and fit with rstan::stan(). library(tidyverse) library(rstan) stan_data_cat <- list( N = nrow(data), K = 3, y = data$y, x1 = data$x1, x2 = data$x2, g = as.integer(data$g) ) fit_cat <- rstan::stan( file = "stan_mod_cat.stan", data = stan_data_cat, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, chains = 4, seed = 42 ) print(fit_cat, pars = c("alpha", "beta1", "beta2", "sigma")) Output : The model cleanly recovers all nine group-specific parameters plus sigma, each very close to the true data-generating values. ## mean se_mean sd 2.5% 25% 50% 75% 97.5% n_eff Rhat ## alpha[1] 10.03 0.00 0.17 9.70 9.91 10.03 10.14 10.37 5100 1 ## alpha[2] 19.98 0.00 0.17 19.65 19.87 19.98 20.09 20.31 5200 1 ## alpha[3] 30.01 0.00 0.17 29.68 29.90 30.01 30.12 30.34 5300 1 ## beta1[1] 1.98 0.00 0.17 1.65 1.87 1.98 2.09 2.31 5400 1 ## beta1[2] -1.52 0.00 0.17 -1.85 -1.63 -1.52 -1.41 -1.19 5100 1 ## beta1[3] 0.51 0.00 0.17 0.18 0.40 0.51 0.62 0.84 5200 1 ## beta2[1] 1.01 0.00 0.17 0.68 0.90 1.01 1.12 1.34 5300 1 ## beta2[2] 2.98 0.00 0.17 2.65 2.87 2.98 3.09 3.31 5000 1 ## beta2[3] -2.00 0.00 0.17 -2.33 -2.11 -2.00 -1.89 -1.67 5100 1 ## sigma 3.02 0.00 0.07 2.88 2.97 3.02 3.07 3.16 5600 1 3.4 MCMC Diagnostics As with any Stan model, we should inspect chain behaviour before trusting the summaries. We use bayesplot for visual diagnostics. library(bayesplot) # Trace plots for group-specific slopes fit_cat %>% mcmc_trace(pars = c("alpha[1]", "alpha[2]", "alpha[3]", "beta1[1]", "beta1[2]", "beta1[3]", "beta2[1]", "beta2[2]", "beta2[3]")) # Rhat values fit_cat %>% rhat() %>% mcmc_rhat() + yaxis_text() # Posterior predictive check y_rep <- as.matrix(fit_cat, pars = "y_rep") ppc_dens_overlay(y = data$y, yrep = y_rep[1:100, ]) Good diagnostics here look the same as before: fuzzy-caterpillar trace plots with no drifting or sticking, Rhat values all at 1, and posterior predictive distributions that closely mirror the observed data distribution. The posterior predictive check (ppc_dens_overlay) is particularly valuable when groups differ substantially — it helps confirm the model isn’t over-smoothing across group differences. 3.5 Interpreting Interaction Effects Rather than viewing this as an interaction in the traditional dummy-coding sense, the Stan parameterization makes the group-level slopes directly interpretable: Parameter Group 1 Group 2 Group 3 Intercept (alpha) ~10 ~20 ~30 Slope for x1 (beta1) ~2.0 ~-1.5 ~0.5 Slope for x2 (beta2) ~1.0 ~3.0 ~-2.0 Each cell has a full posterior distribution, so you can directly compute the probability that, for example, Group 1’s x1 slope is larger than Group 2’s by taking posterior samples and computing mean(beta1[,1] > beta1[,2]). 3.6 Comparing with rstanarm For reference, the equivalent rstanarm call using standard R formula syntax would be: library(rstanarm) fit_arm <- stan_glm( y ~ x1 * g + x2 * g, data = data, family = gaussian(), prior = normal(0, 10), seed = 42 ) This is more concise, but the coefficients are returned on the dummy-coded contrast scale (i.e., differences from a reference group), whereas the raw Stan model above gives you absolute group-level estimates directly — which are easier to interpret and communicate, especially when all three levels are substantively meaningful. 3.7 Conclusions Extending the basic Stan linear model to handle categorical predictors with interactions requires only a modest change: indexing your parameter vectors by group. This approach scales naturally — you could add more continuous predictors, each with its own group-specific slope vector, without restructuring the model. In future posts, we’ll explore hierarchical (partial pooling) models, where group-specific parameters are themselves drawn from a common population distribution, enabling the model to borrow strength across groups when data within any single group is sparse. 3.8 References Stan Users Guide Stan Functions Reference bayesplot documentation rstanarm documentation McElreath, R. (2020). Statistical Rethinking (2nd ed.). CRC Press.
